text: The surgeon begins a laparoscopic myomectomy by controlling the uterine arteries at their origin. The peritoneum is opened between the round ligament and the IP ligament using an ultrasonic scalpel. In the retroperitoneal space, the internal iliac artery is identified laterally and the uterus medially. The pararectal fossa is opened with lateral traction, followed by the medial paramedical fossa, bordered by the bladder medially and the umbilical artery laterally. The internal iliac artery, umbilical artery, and the origin of the uterine artery are clearly visible.

The surgeon places a clip on the origin of the right uterine artery to reduce blood flow to the uterus. The anatomy is clearly visible, including the internal iliac artery laterally and the ureter medially. The assistant retracts the left IP ligament medially, allowing clear visualization of the retroperitoneal space. Using an ultrasonic device, the surgeon dissects between the ureter and the internal iliac artery, developing the lateral paravesical fossa. The left iliac forceps are used to manipulate the umbilical artery, revealing the uterine artery as the medial trunk of the internal iliac artery. The anatomy, including the uterine artery, umbilical artery, paramedical fossa, and ureter, is clearly seen. A clip is placed on the origin of the left uterine artery.

The surgeon uses the harmonic ACE in cutting mode to open the myometrial serosa and longitudinally incise the uterus, aiming to minimize perioperative bleeding. The myoma is tracked with ten millimeter forceps to enhance visibility of the dissection plane. The harmonic ACE cuts the tissue with precise visual control, and the uterus is opened to seven centimeters for a ten centimeter myoma. The forceps are repositioned laterally to improve retraction and visibility, ensuring the dissection is bloodless and focused on the fibroid rather than the uterine myometrium.

The surgeon cuts tissue directly in contact with the myoma, avoiding the residual endometrial material. The myomectomy is nearly complete, with minimal bleeding observed. The intracavitary myoma is controlled, and endometrial polyps are identified for removal using the harmonic device. The endometrial cavity is visible, and hemostasis is initiated.

The surgeon uses a 14mm needle to perform intracorporeal knotting, placing stitches 1.5 cm outside the uterine scar to invaginate healthy myometrium into the myoma bed for improved hemostasis. The assistant aids in retracting and invaginating the tissue, while the surgeon locks the knot without using bipolar cautery.

The surgeon uses the crocodile technique to invaginate healthy myometrium into the myoma bed, securing the tissue with a double flat knot. The assistant aids in cranial traction of the tissue, ensuring both edges of the suture are well aligned. The final knot is placed above the previous one, avoiding the right and left tubal cords, achieving uterine reconstruction and hemostasis without bipolar cautery.

The surgeon inserts a 15mm morcellator through the left iliac operative trocar to perform morcellation of the myoma. The morcellation process is completed in less than three minutes, followed by rinsing the abdominal cavity to inspect the uterine scar.
The surgeon controls both uterine arteries at their origin, with the pelvis appearing completely free of blood. The uterine scar is visible and well-formed.
